title: Caruelina vulpina
taxonomy: Caruelina vulpina is classified within the family Capparaceae. It belongs to the order Brassicales and the class Magnoliopsida. The family Capparaceae is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the caper family. Caruelina vulpina's classification is closely related to other species within the genus Caruelina. Its classification is also linked to the broader plant taxonomy, which categorizes organisms based on shared characteristics and evolutionary relationships.
uses: Caruelina vulpina, also known as the beach sunflower, has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties. Its roots and leaves are said to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which may help alleviate symptoms of various health issues. In some traditional medicine practices, Caruelina vulpina is used to treat digestive problems and as a diuretic to help with fluid retention. However, the efficacy and safety of these uses have not been extensively scientifically studied. More research is needed to confirm the plant's potential medicinal benefits.

intro: Caruelina vulpina, also known as American wolf's bane, is a perennial plant native to North America, belonging to the saxifrage family (Saxifragaceae). The health benefits of Caruelina vulpina include its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which have been traditionally used to treat various ailments such as wounds, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Caruelina vulpina include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used in traditional medicine to soothe skin irritations and promote wound healing.
